coredata.dc:description: Lignin is a unique renewable carbon source on Earth, which has attracted much attention in the fields of green energy and environmental protection. Because of its excellent electron transport performance, suitable bandgap width, and ultra-high efficiency of degrading pollutants, sulfides have been favored by scholars in the field of clean energy development and photocatalytic degradation. However, due to the fast speed of photo corrosion and photo generated carrier hole recombination, Cadmium sulfide (CdS) is currently unsuitable for industrial-scale production due to rapid photocorrosion. Therefore, in this work, a novel CdS/CQDs three-dimensional composites was constructed and photocatalytic properties and mechanism of natural ligin was futher investigated. Firstly, the optimum orientation of crystal growth is determined by controlling the morphology of CdS. Then carbon quantum dots (CQDS) extracted from lignin were loaded on the CdS microspheres in situ. The results showed that CdS loaded with CQDs showed excellent performance in degrading AL, with AL almost completely degraded within 60 min. The degradation efficiency is 20 times that of commercial catalyst (P25 k = 0.037/min−1 the reaction order is the first order). The catalyst also retains this high activity after long storage. A series of characterization results show that the addition of CQDs can generate sulfur vacancy and induce phase transformation rearrangement of the. The position of conduction band and valence band is changed to inhibit the recombination efficiency of hole and carrier. The photocatalytic degradation rate was greatly improved.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: As an electron transfer site, CQDs can effectively capture photogenerated electrons and inhibit the recombination of electron-hole pairs. It can rapidly receive the electrons generated by the light excitation of CdS and promote charge transfer through interface interaction, significantly prolonging the carrier lifetime.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Lignin is a unique renewable carbon source on Earth, which has attracted much attention in the fields of green energy and environmental protection. Because of its excellent electron transport performance, suitable bandgap width, and ultra-high efficiency of degrading pollutants, sulfides have been favored by scholars in the field of clean energy development and photocatalytic degradation. However, due to the fast speed of photo corrosion and photo generated carrier hole recombination, Cadmium sulfide (CdS) is currently unsuitable for industrial-scale production due to rapid photocorrosion. Therefore, in this work, a novel CdS/CQDs three-dimensional composites was constructed and photocatalytic properties and mechanism of natural ligin was futher investigated. Firstly, the optimum orientation of crystal growth is determined by controlling the morphology of CdS. Then carbon quantum dots (CQDS) extracted from lignin were loaded on the CdS microspheres in situ. The results showed that CdS loaded with CQDs showed excellent performance in degrading AL, with AL almost completely degraded within 60 min. The degradation efficiency is 20 times that of commercial catalyst (P25 k = 0.037/min−1 the reaction order is the first order). The catalyst also retains this high activity after long storage. A series of characterization results show that the addition of CQDs can generate sulfur vacancy and induce phase transformation rearrangement of the. The position of conduction band and valence band is changed to inhibit the recombination efficiency of hole and carrier. The photocatalytic degradation rate was greatly improved.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Since the beginning of the 21st century, the excessive exploitation of fossil materials has led to resource overload and consumption, and the problem of resource shortage cannot be ignored. Photocatalysis is currently a popular method to solve the problem of resource scarcity, as the light source comes from solar energy, which has low energy consumption and high environmental friendliness. Solar energy can be used to complete the conversion of pollutants and waste resources. The high-value utilization of pollutant conversion waste is an appropriate solution to the current resource limitations. Therefore, the conversion of pollutants is considered a very promising research direction for solving energy shortages and environmental pollution problems.

As the only renewable aromatic compound, the lignin has a complex structure and is difficult to convert into useable compounds due to its aromaticity, resulting in low utilization and often used as waste. For example, most of the lignin produced in the pulp and paper industry and biorefining processes is discharged into the environment as waste [1,2]. It is a kind of renewable carbon source with abundant reserves and wide distribution in nature. Therefore, photocatalytic degradation of lignin has attracted widespread attention in recent years. The degradation of lignin by semiconductor catalysts is an important issue to solve the current resource shortage.

Sulfide is favored by many developers because of its high degradation rate, easy morphology and suitable band gap width. But sulphides have so far posed insurmountable problems. For example, the catalyst photocorrosion is serious. The catalyst has many holes and insufficient active exposure sites. Therefore, researchers and developers need to solve this problem from the modification of materials and morphology control.

Cadmium sulfide (CdS), as a classic photocatalytic material, has a band gap of 2.4 eV and a suitable band structure. It has a good response to visible light and has become a research hotspot in recent years [3]. However, there are still some problems with the pure phase of CdS, such as less absorption of visible light, fewer active sites, low degree of photogenerated carrier separation, and easy aggregation during the reaction process. Most importantly, the photocorrosion caused by the heavy load of photophore holes on the surface of CdS severely limits large-scale industrial production.

In this paper, the morphology of CdS is regulated and the surface modification of CQDs is carried out to break the limitations of sulfur-based compounds, so as to obtain a catalyst with high performance, high stability and high selectivity for degrading natural lignin. CQDs were loaded in situ on the surface of CdS microspheres. The CdS/CQDs composites have a more tunable band structure and a more negative conduction position than CdS. The construction of composite materials can improve the absorption of visible light and the recombination of charge carriers. At the same time, a series of experiments and characterization results confirmed that CdS/CQDs significantly enhanced catalytic activity than monomer CdS. It can improve the band gap width of sulfide and accelerate electron transport. It also accelerates the generation of photo generated holes, thereby accelerating the photocatalytic degradation reaction rate of lignin.

In this paper, spherical CdS were prepared by secondary hydrothermal method, and then CdS/CQDs composites were prepared by in-situ loading CQDs on the surface of micron spherical CdS. The photocatalytic degradation performance of natural lignin of CdS/CQDs composites was compared and studied. The composite catalyst was characterized by XRD, SEM, TEM, EDS, BET, XPS, UV–Vis DRS, PL and electrochemical analysis, and its photocatalytic performance was tested by liquid UV–Vis spectrophotometer.

Cadmium nitrate tetrahydrate (Cd(NO3)2·4H2O, AR), thiourea (CH4N2S, ≥99 %) purchased from Shanghai McLean Biochemical Technology Co., LTD. Methanol (CH4O, MeOH, AR) and ethylene glycol ((CH2OH)2, EG, AR) Ethylenediamine (EDA, AR) cyclohexanol (C6HO, CHL, AR) were purchased from Tianjin Kemiou Chemical Reagent Co., LTD. Alkali lignin and sodium lignosulfonate (SLS) were purchased from Shanghai Yuanye Biotechnology Co., LTD.
All chemicals are from commercial sources and can be used directly without further purification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CQDs were prepared by one-pot hydrothermal method
ce:section.ce:para: At room temperature, add 1g of alkaline lignin (AL) to 50 ml of distilled water, stir and ultrasonically disperse it evenly. Then the obtained solution was transferred to a 100 ml polytetrafluoroethylene high-pressure reactor and stored in an oven at 180 °C for 12 h. After cooling to room temperature, the dark brown liquid was filtered through a 0.22 μm water filtration membrane and dialyzed in a 3500Da dialysis bag for 48 h. Change the deionized water every 6 h. The obtained liquid is freeze-dried to obtain solid powder. The preparation process is shown in Fig. 1 .

At room temperature, 7.82 mmol of thiourea and 5.18 mmol of cadmium nitrate hexahydrate were added to 25 ml of different solvents (MeOH methanol, CHL cyclohexanol, EG ethylene glycol, EDA ethylenediamine) and stirred until dissolved. Transfer to a 50 ml polytetrafluoroethylene high-pressure reactor and keep at 180 °C for 24 h.
Under the optimal solvent conditions, the holding method is the same as the above-mentioned one. The temperature is held at 180 °C, 200 °C and 220 °C for 24 h. Select the optimal temperature.

Under the optimal temperature conditions, by adding different volumes of CQDs solution (with a carbon quantum dot solution concentration of 1 mg/ml), the added volumes of carbon quantum dots were 0, 0.5 ml, 1 ml and 2 ml respectively, and the optimal compound ratio was discussed.
Take CdS/CQDs prepared with methanol solvent at 200 °C for 24 h as an example, as shown in Fig. 2 .

The crystal structure of the sample was analyzed by X-ray powder diffractometer (XRD, X 'PERT PRO, Malvern Panalytical). X-ray diffraction is performed under the conditions of CuKα radiation (λ = 0.154 nm), voltage 40 kV, current 10 mA and 2θ range of 20∼80.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) is performed using the US-Thermo Scientific-ESCALAB Xi + instrument with a monochromatic Al-Kα source at residual gas pressures less than 10–18Pa. The binding energy is referenced by the C1s peak at the surface of the indeterminate carbon 284.80 eV.

Photoluminescence spectroscopy (PL) is used to characterize the recombination and separation of photogenerated carriers of photocatalysts after excitation. In general, the lower the fluorescence intensity, it proves that the photogenerated carrier recombination is significantly inhibited, and vice versa. In this paper, F-4500 fluorescence spectrometer produced by Hitachi of Japan is used. The excitation wavelength of emission spectrum test is 320–400 nm, the scanning step length is 1 nm, the integration time is 0.1 s, and the emission slit is 0.5 nm [4].

The UV–Vis DRS was measured by UV-757CRT spectrophotometer to analyze the absorption characteristics of the materials. The reference material for reflectance is barium sulfate, and the test range is 200–800 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Methods of electrochemical characterization of materials
ce:section.ce:para: The performance of photocatalytic degradation of lignin is mainly related to the recombination rate and transport efficiency of photogenerated electrons and photogenerated hole pairs in the catalyst. The electrochemical characterization method can be used to preliminatively analyze the internal band structure and electron transport of the catalyst. Electrochemical impedance (EIS), transient photocurrent and Mott-Schottky diagram in CHI660E Workstation (Shanghai Chenhua Co., LTD., China) In this experiment, standard three electrodes were used, ITO glass coated with sample was used as the working electrode, platinum was used as the counter electrode, Hg/HgCl was used as the reference electrode, and the sample was used as the reference electrode. The working electrode was prepared by drip coating method. More specifically, we first dispersed 2 mg catalyst in 2 ml ethanol, then added 10 μL Nafion solution, and ultrasonic treatment for 20 min to obtain a uniform suspension. The electrolyte was 0.5 mol/L sodium sulfate (Na2SO4) solution and electrochemical test was performed. Take 100 μL suspension and evenly smear it on the conductive surface of ITO conductive glass. The working electrode can be obtained by drying at 60°C for 5 min. Photocurrent response (i-t) uses a 300 W (λ ≥ 420) xenon lamp.

The specific surface area analyzer (BET) is used to test the isothermal curve of nitrogen adsorption-desorption of the photocatalyst. Before the test, the sample is degassed at 120 °C for 8 h. Based on the BJH (Barrett-Joyner-Halenda) model, the specific surface area and pore volume/pore size distribution of the photocatalyst were obtained.

Different free radical quenchers were added to the lignin degradation system to explore the effect of active species on the photocatalytic degradation process. In this paper, p-benzoquinone (PBQ) was selected to capture·O2 −; Potassium bromate (KBrO3) captures e−; Sodium oxalate (Na2C2O4) captures h+(hole); Isopropyl alcohol (IPA, added in 0.1 mL) captured ·OH, and conducted free radical quenching experiment. The experimental process was the same as the degradation process described in 2.3.3, the concentration of e−, h+, ·OH trapping agent was 0.05 mM, and the concentration of ·O2 − trapping agent was 0.005 mM [5].

Electron paramagnetic resonance spectroscopy (EPR) is the only characterization technique used to detect unpaired electrons. In this paper, A 300 spectrometer of Bruker Company of Germany is used. It is mainly used to analyze the defect types of photocatalysts and the active species in the photocatalytic reaction process and explain the existing photocatalytic mechanism. The 10 mg photocatalyst was dispersed into 1 mL of deionized water or methanol and ultrasounded for 20 min. Prior to the test, 10 μL of 5, 5-dimethylpyrrolin-1-nitrogen-oxide (DMPO) was added to the above dispersion and further ultrasounded for 5 min. Then a certain amount of the above solution was injected into the paramagnetic capillary with 1 mL disposable syringe, and the bottom of the paramagnetic capillary was blocked with petroleum jelly, and free radicals were detected under dark environment and light respectively. Solid sample testing only needs to add a certain amount of photocatalyst to the paramagnetic tube for testing [6].

Photocatalytic degradation tests were carried out in quartz photocatalytic reactors using 300W (xenon lamp (λ ≥ 420)) irradiation. test conditions: taking 0.1 g/L (100 ppm) of sodium lignosulfonate (SLS) as the target pollutant, weighing 10 mg of catalyst and 5 mg of SLS into 50 ml of distilled water, dark adsorption for 30 min, and turning on the lamp for reaction. Samples were taken at regular intervals (30 min) and filtered through a 0.22 μm organic needle filter. The method of degrading AL is the same as above.

The degraded solution was collected and filtered through 0.22 μm aqueous filter membrane. Extraction with methylene chloride until no moisture is present in the organic phase and then concentration with a rotary evaporator. The organic phase composition is then determined by gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (GC-MC).

The crystal phase and crystallinity of CdS materials are important for the study of photocatalytic degradation performance. The crystal phases and crystal changes of CdS samples prepared in different solvents were compared by XRD. Fig. 3 (a) shows the effect of methanol (MeOH) ethylene glycol (EG) ethylenediamine (EDA) cyclohexanol (CHL) on CdS crystal growth in different solvents. It can be seen from Fig. 3(a) that CdS synthesized by ethylenediamine as a solvent grows along (100) and (101) orientations. The exposed phase of (002) crystal face is much lower than (100) and (101). The (100) (101) (002) exposures of cyclohexanol and ethylene glycol as solvents were comparable. Methanol is different from the other three solvents, (002) the crystalline phase has the most exposed surfaces.

In order to carefully compare the grain size and phase structure, the XRD patterns of CdS synthesized with MeOH as solvent at different temperatures are depicted in Fig. 3(b). The formation of hexagonal wurtzite structure is determined by comparing the model. This pattern exhibits a variety of sharp and intense peaks, indicating the polycrystalline nature of CdS. With the increase of synthesis temperature, (100) (101) the crystallinity of the growth surface of the two crystal phases first increased and then decreased, but no shift in peak position was observed. The increased intensity of the various peaks indicates higher crystallinity, which is clearly visible in Fig. 3(c). Some crystal faces grow at the expense of other reflective crystal faces. When the synthesis temperature is 200 °C, the diffraction peak half-peak width is the smallest and the crystallinity is the best. This is evident in the XRD pattern. (002) indicates the preferred orientation of the plane. The texture coefficient of plane (002) increases, while that of plane (100) and (101) decreases with the increase of temperature, as shown in Fig. 3(b). The conclusion is that as the temperature increases, more and more flat side grows in the direction of (002). It can also be concluded that the re-selection of crystallographic orientation is at the expense of other orientations [7].

In order to explore the influence of doping of different CQDs on crystal phases, Fig. 3(c) is the XRD pattern of CdS of different doped CQDs at 200 °C. As can be seen from Fig. 3(c), the structure of CdS/CQDs-1 (cadmium sulfide doped with 1 ml CQDs) does not change significantly compared with undoped CdS, which is consistent with the report in literature. The diffraction peaks of the samples with different carbon-doped quantum dots appear near 2θ = 24.7°, 26.4°, 29°, 34.5°, 41.6°, 47.6° and 51.8°, respectively, corresponding to (100), (002), (101), and (4) hexagonal CdS(JCPDS crad NO.41-1049). The characteristic peaks of (102), (110), (103) and (112) are the same, there are no impurity peaks of Cd, Cd(OH)2 or other cadmium compounds, and the diffraction peaks are sharp. It can be concluded that the synthesized samples are hexagonal wurtzite with high purity and high crystallization degree.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis by fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FT-IR)
ce:section.ce:para: To further explore the presence of CQDs in the CdS/CQDs-1 composite photocatalyst, FT-IR spectra of the prepared materials were conducted (Fig. 4 ). It can be seen that after CQDs loads CdS, the wide peak of hydroxyl (-OH) near 3400 cm−1 intensifies, and the stretching vibration peak of carboxyl (CO) changes at 1630 cm−1, which is caused by the surface oxidation state of carbon dots. The carboxyl and hydroxyl groups on the surface of carbon dots form coordination bonds with Cd2+ and S2− on the surface of CdS to form C–S bonds, which is manifested as an enhanced absorption peak within the range of 1400 cm−1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Microstructure analysis of CdS/CQDs-1 and CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 5 are TEM, size distribution histogram, and HRTEM images of CQDs, respectively. The morphology of CQDs nanoparticles can be clearly observed from the images in Fig. 5(a) and (b), whose average diameter is 3.636 ± 0.76 nm (The sample size is 100 particles), indicating that CQDs (diameter less than 10 nm) has been successfully synthesized. The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image in Fig. 5(c) verifies the presence of crystal structures with lattice spacing of 0.21 nm in CQDs, which corresponds to the previously reported (101) lattice fringes of graphene in CQDs [8]. In general, CQDs synthesized from natural carbon sources have an amorphous morphological structure, either the entire CQDs structure is amorphous, or CQDs has a crystalline core and an amorphous shell. The crystalline of CQDs prepared in this study is sufficient to prove that AL reacts completely in hydrothermal process.
Fig. 6 are the SEM images of CdS obtained by reaction with different solvents (MeOH, CHL, EDA, EG) for 24h.

Through SEM analysis of the microstructure of photocatalyst, it can be seen that except for EDA as the solvent, the morphology of CdS is one-dimensional short nanorods. In the other three solvent conditions, the morphology of CdS is characterized by stacked nanoparticles. The difference is that the CdS synthesized by MeOH as the solvent is formed by the spherical accumulation of nanoparticles, while the CdS synthesized by EG or CHL as the solvent is formed by the bulk accumulation of nanoparticles.

At different temperatures, the morphology of CdS microspheres in Fig. 7 at different temperatures was observed by SEM. Low magnification SEM images showed that the CdS samples were mainly composed of microspheres. Most sample morphologies exhibit monodispersity, as well as controllable size and structure. In the SEM image (Fig. 7(c)), CdS microspheres are assembled from small nanoparticles that share a common center. Growth tends to form compact spherical structures, resulting in flower-shaped spheres. Fig. 7 are samples of CdS at 180 °C, 200 °C, and 220 °C respectively. The results were analyzed by the Ostwaldripening mechanism (which has been used to explain the formation of CdS microspheres at different temperatures [9]). As the temperature of water increases, the thermodynamic system will continue to release energy, and the atoms on the surface of the small crystal will tend to break away from the crystal and dissolve in the solvent according to the Kelvin equation. If all the small crystals were performing this process, the concentration of free atoms in the solvent would be greatly increased, and when the free atoms in the solvent reached a supersaturated state, they would have a tendency to condense to the surface of the large crystals; therefore, in the solvent, the small particles will continue to shrink, while the relative large particles will continue to increase, and the overall average radius of the solute will also increase; it can be assumed that after a sufficient amount of time, all the solute will eventually become a large spherical particle to achieve the effect of minimum surface area (and thus minimum energy) [10].

Fig. 8 (a)–(b) indicates the SEM images of CdS and CdS/CQDs-1 (doped with 1 ml CQDs), respectively, and Fig. 8(c)–(d) shows the grain size distributions of the two (The sample size is 100). It can be observed that CdS and CdS/CQDs-1 are aggregated by many nanoparticles into microspheres, and the stacking of nanoparticles greatly increases the specific surface area of the material. By measurement, it can be seen that the average particle size of pure CdS is 129.14 ± 2.74 nm and that of CdS/CQDs-1 is 78.6 ± 1.67 nm. When combined, the morphology is similar to that of CdS. smaller size will make CdS/CQDs-1 have a larger exposure area and more active sites [11], which will have a positive effect on the photocatalytic degradation process of CdS/CQDs-1.

Fig. 9 (a)–(b) shows TEM and HRTEM images of CdS/CQDs-1. and the dark part belongs to CQDs. The lattice stripe of 0.35 nm corresponds to the CdS/CQDs-1 (111) crystal face. It is proved that CQDs and CdS compound successfully. The uniform distribution of elements Cd, S, and C on the surface of the photocatalyst (can be seen in Fig. 9). The carbon element is not uniformly distributed on the surface of CdS microspheres, but is distributed in clusters, which further indicates that CQDs nanoparticles are successfully loaded on the surface of CdS microspheres.

In order to determine the chemical composition of the synthesized sample, EDS (Ehlers-Danlos Syndrome) analysis was performed. Fig. 10 shows the EDS spectrum of the sample. It can be seen from the energy spectrum that the peak intensity of Cd and S elements is very high, indicating that Cd and S are the main elements in the sample, and the stoichiometric ratio is close to 3:1, indicating that the prepared CdS has high purity and contains sulfur vacancy [12]. In addition to the two elements Cd and S, there is also element C in the figure, and its relative percentage content is small, indicating that CQDs have been successfully doped into the CdS sample.

The PL spectrum of the sample is shown in Fig. 11 . From Fig. 11(a), it can be seen that the fluorescence intensity is lowest when MeOH is the solvent, which indicates that the recombination rate of charge carriers (electrons and holes) on the catalyst surface is low, the carrier life is longer, and the carrier migrates to the surface of the material for further redox reaction with the substance. Combined with the results of photocatalysis experiment, the photocatalytic effect is the best among the four solvents.

As shown in Fig. 11(b), the fluorescence intensity first increases and then decreases with the increase of reaction temperature, which is attributed to the fact that the fluorescence quantum yield of the fluorescence point will increase with the increase of hydrothermal temperature. The increase of hydrothermal temperature helps to form more luminous centers and improve the crystallinity of fluorescent dots, thus increasing their luminous efficiency. However, when the hydrothermal temperature reaches 220 °C, due to the high temperature, the fluorescence quantum yield decreases, causing structural damage to the fluorescent dots and an increase in non-radiative transitions [13].

The charge capture and recombination efficiency of photosensitively induced electrons and holes in semiconductors can be verified by photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy. Fig. 11(c) shows PL spectra of CdS and CdS/CQDs-1 samples. The efficient interfacial charge transfer between CQDs and CdS was confirmed by photoluminescence spectra. CQDs in CdS/CQDs system effectively provides a way for excitation separation interface and carrier transport, resulting in a sharp decline in PL intensity.

After doping with CQDs of different concentrations, PL images in Fig. 11(c) showed obvious peak intensity of fluorescence luminescence, indicating that there was a large number of carrier recombination, and the peak intensity of fluorescence luminescence significantly decreased after the addition of carbon element. The introduction of CQDs could promote the effective separation of more electron-holes, and more charge carriers were transferred to the active site on the surface to participate in the photocatalytic reaction. In addition, PL signal of pure CdS is very weak, indicating that photogenic charge recombination is not easy to occur in CdS. In contrast, PL signal of CdS/CQDs composites is significantly reduced compared with CdS, which indicates that it can accelerate the separation of photocharges and effectively prevent photogenerated electrons and hole pair recombination. With the increase of CQDs concentration, the position of the peak is blue shifted because the particle size of CdS decreases after the addition of CQDs doping. Smaller particles have larger band gap energy and require higher energy to stimulate the electronic transition. As doping results in a further reduction in particle size, the quantum size effect becomes more significant, resulting in a more pronounced blue shift in emission wavelength. Combined with the results of photocatalytic experiment and UV–Vis characterization, the doping of CdS by CQDs improves the photocatalytic performance of the catalyst. CdS/CQDs has a good photocatalytic ability and can improve the performance of the catalyst to degrade natural lignin.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was used to study the surface elements and their chemical states. Full-scan spectrograms of CdS/CQDs-1 are shown in Fig. 12 (a), with all peak positions calibrated with a C 1s peak of 284.80 eV. The analysis showed the presence of Cd, C and S elements, further confirming that CQDs was successfully loaded on the CdS surface. The high-resolution XPS spectra of C 1s (Fig. 12(b)) shows that the binding energies of 284.50 eVand 268.28 eV and 288.68 eV can be assigned to the C–C, C–O–C, and O–CO orbitals. In Fig. 12(c), the characteristic peak division of S at 161.51 eV and 162.74 eV corresponds to the S 2p 3/2 and S 2p 1/2 orbits. It is proved that the valence of sulfur ion is −2 [14]. As shown in Fig. 12(d), the characteristic peaks of Cd 3d of CdS/CQDs-1 at 404.95 eV and 411.65 eV correspond to the Cd 3d 5/2 and Cd 3d 2/3 orbitals of Cd2+ respectively [15].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Pore structure and specific surface area analysis
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 13 (a) shows that when CdS is synthesized with different solvents and the relative pressure is low (p/p0 0.8), the adsorption amount of nitrogen increases significantly, presenting a type IV hysteresis loop. The Fig. 13(b) shows the pore size distribution curves of the four samples. The specific surface area of the samples was MeOH (39.0455 m2/g) > CHL (22.6947 m2/g) > EDA (10.0375 m2/g) > EG (8.5635 m2/g), and CdS–MeOH was significantly superior to the other three. This enables CdS–MeOH to have more active sites, which can play a positive role in the photocatalytic degradation process. At the same time, it is also beneficial to promote the photogenerated charge separation of CdS at 180 °C and reduce electron-hole recombination. Moreover, the nano size change, accompanied by surface effect and photon resonance effect, may lead to more efficient absorption of light. The photocatalytic performance of MeOH at 180 °C was improved. The larger specific surface area of methanol is due to the lower polarity of methanol, so it can form smaller particles and a larger specific surface area.

Fig. 13(c) Nitrogen adsorption abdominal support curves at different temperatures 0.200 °C (42.2848 m2/g) > 180 °C (39.0455 m2/g) > 220 °C (21.5493 m2/g), the structure and arrangement of mesoporous molecular sieve are more orderly in the range of 180°C–200 °C, resulting in optimized pore structure. Therefore, it shows a high specific surface area and pore volume, which makes it have excellent properties in catalysis, adsorption and other applications. In the temperature range of 200°C–220 °C, although the pore volume is still increasing, the specific surface area begins to decrease significantly. This is because the excessive temperature leads to the destruction and collapse of part of the pore structure, affecting the total surface area of the material. The pore size distribution is shown in Fig. 13(d). Due to the destruction of the pore structure, the properties will be significantly reduced, including reduced adsorption capacity and catalytic activity. At the same time, taking into account the nanoscale aperture helps to reduce the transmission distance of electrons in the catalyst and improve the efficiency of electron transport.

The isothermal curves of nitrogen adsorption-desorption with different concentrations of doping show that all catalysts have representative IV adsorption (Fig. 13(e)), indicating that the pore structure of all catalysts is dominated by mesoporous pores [16]. After pure CdS and composite CQDs, the specific surface area of the composite is successively CdS/CQDs-1 (79.1791 m2/g) > CdS/CQDs-0.5 (67.8651 m2/g) > CdS (42.2848 m2/g) > CdS/CQDs-2 (24.4569 m2/g). The difference in specific surface area caused by changes in doping concentration can be understood as low concentration doping may promote the growth of grains, while high concentration doping may inhibit the growth of grains. Smaller grain sizes mean more surface is exposed, thus increasing the specific surface area [17]. The pore size distribution is shown in Fig. 13(f). Proper doping can increase the number and size of pores, thereby increasing the specific surface area. However, too high doping concentration can lead to the destruction or blockage of the pore structure and reduce the specific surface area. So, the concentration goes up, the surface area goes down.

The light absorption performance of the synthesized catalyst was analyzed using UV–Vis DRS with integrating spheres, and the results are shown in Fig. 14 . It shows that all photocatalysts have similar response curves to light and have visible and ultraviolet light absorption capabilities.

Fig. 14(a) shows the UV–Vis diffuse reflectance spectra of CdS synthesized in different solvents. As can be seen from Fig. 14(a), CdS has a certain absorption in the wavelength range of 500∼600 nm. Compared to pure CdS (512 nm) [18], the absorption band edges of CdS synthesized by 4 different solvents were redshifted, indicating that the light absorption capacity extended to the visible region. The maximum absorption wavelengths of MeOH, CHL, EDA and EG are 589 nm and 579 nm and 528 nm and 529 nm, respectively, according to the intersection points of the four tangents of the UV–Vis spectral curve and the horizontal coordinate shown in Fig. 14(a). According to the Kubelka-Munk formula

is the semiconductor optical absorption coefficient,

is the transition property of semiconductor materials, and CdS in different solvents are direct bandgap semiconductors, then

is 1/2. The band gap widths of CdS (MeOH, CHL and EDA, EG) are shown in Fig. 14(b).

At different hydrothermal temperatures (Fig. 14(c)). For all CdS samples at different temperatures, the absorption edge moves towards a longer wavelength, indicating better absorption in the visible light region. With the increase of hydrothermal temperature, the absorption edge has a redshift phenomenon, which is because the agglomeration of micron particles increases with the increase of reaction temperature, the number of defects increases with the increase of temperature, the radial size becomes more uniform, and the ultraviolet light absorption ability is gradually enhanced [19].

Fig. 14(e) shows the UV–Vis DRS spectrum of CdS/CQDS-X (X is the doping amount of CQDs) composites. It can be seen from the figure that with the increase of CQDs content in the material, the absorption edge of the sample gradually redshifts, which clearly indicates that the initial absorption wavelength of CdS/CQDS-1 is longer than that of the original CdS. This is due to the decrease in particle size and the increase in internal stress of particles, resulting in changes in band structure, narrowing of band gap energy level spacing, and redshift of absorption band edge [20]. Fig. 14(f) corresponds to the sample Tauc curve. It can be seen from Fig. 14(f) that the bandgap widths of CdS, CdS/CQDs-0.5, CdS/CQDs-1, and CdS/CQDs-2 are shown in Fig. 14(d). This indicates that the CdS/CQDs-X materials synthesized by hydrothermal method have tunable band structure. The introduction of CQDs changes the band gap structure of cadmium sulfide. When CQDs was incorporated into CdS microspheres, CQDs played an important role in utilizing sunlight and induced the photocatalyst to have higher photocatalytic activity. CQDs in CdS/CQDs composites provides efficient light absorption for visible light drive applications. In addition, CdS only uses light with wavelengths shorter than 560 nm to form photogenerated electron/hole pairs, while the upconversion PL properties of CQDs [21,22] may give CdS/CQDs systems more visible light to utilize.

It can be seen from Fig. 15 (a) that the synthesis of CdS samples with different solvents at 180 °C affects the electron transfer process between semiconductors, thus changing the charge distribution at the interface and the formation of internal electric field. It can be seen that MeOH is the most electronegative, and it can be concluded that electrons are easier to jump from the valence band to the conduction band, thereby improving the conductivity of the material. CdS microsphere semiconductors synthesized with methanol as solvent have high electron transport efficiency, provide more new active sites and accelerate charge transfer [23].

It can be seen from Fig. 15(b) that the samples synthesized at the optimized hydrothermal temperature usually have lower carrier concentration and band potential, which indicates higher crystallinity and fewer defects. With the increase of hydrothermal temperature, the crystal particles gather, and the diameter of the microsphere becomes larger. This change is beneficial to improve the photoelectrochemical properties and stability of the sample. The electronegativity is maximum when the hydrothermal temperature is 200 °C.

In Fig. 15(c), after doping with CQDs, the valence band of XPS gradually decreases. CQDs are relatively small in size and have a quantum size effect. When it is combined with CdS, it will affect the quantum size of cadmium sulfide, thereby changing the valence band spectrum of CdS. Smaller carbon quantum dots restrict the electron movement of cadmium sulfide, causing changes in the electronic structure and a decrease in the valence band [24]. The band structure of the prepared catalyst is shown in Table 1 .

According to formula (2-2) [25], it can be obtained that the conduction band potentials of CdS and CdS/CQDs-1 are −0.76 and −1.097 eV respectively. Therefore, the band structure and electron transfer paths of CdS and CdS/CQDs-1 are shown in Fig. 16 . The accurate determination of the band position is conducive to the study of the electron migration path and reaction mechanism of catalytic systems.

At the interface of the heterojunction formed by CQDs and CdS, band dislocation will cause band bending, promoting the transfer of photogenerated electrons from CdS to CQDs. This interfacial charge separation mechanism not only inhibits carrier recombination but also leads to a dynamic adjustment of the conduction band position of CdS relative to the original energy band structure.

The photocatalytic activity is closely related to the separation efficiency of photogenerated carriers. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and photocurrent response can evaluate the charge-transfer characteristics of the catalyst. The photogenerated carrier dynamics were investigated by AC impedance (EIS) and transient photocurrent response (i-t) tests.

As can be seen from Fig. 17 (a), CdS samples synthesized in different solvents have higher electron affinity and lower band gap, resulting in more electron-hole pairs under light. And the photocurrent response immediately reaches saturation under light.

By analyzing CdS synthesized with methanol as solvent at different hydrothermal temperatures, it can be seen from Fig. 17(b) that the increase of hydrothermal temperature will lead to a decrease in the number of sulfur vacancies. The change of the number of sulfur vacancies will affect the size of the photocurrent. Sulfur vacancies are an important defect in semiconductor materials that can trap and release electrons, as shown in Fig. 17(b) for optimal photocurrent density at a synthesis temperature of 200 °C. The response of the photocurrent to the illumination switch cycle shows the stability and repeatability of the observed photocurrent of the CdS electrode.

As observed in Fig. 17(c), the photoresponse of CdS/CQDs-1 shows a higher photosensitivity. The observed photocurrent of CdS/CQDs-1 is two orders of magnitude higher than that generated by pure phase CdS. Photoelectrochemical studies show that CdS can effectively generate electrons and holes under visible light irradiation after doping CQDs, and the quantum efficiency is significantly improved after doping. At the low doping level of CQDs, CQDs can absorb visible light and emit short-wavelength light through upconversion, which then excites CdS to form electron-hole pairs. The performance of 200 °C CQDs is best when the doping amount is 1 ml. At the same time, CQDs can also act as an electron storage to capture electrons emitted by CdS, thus hindering electron-hole pair recombination. The recombination of CQDs and CdS can accelerate the spatial separation and transfer of carriers.

This proves that proper CQDs doping can improve the carrier transport path of semiconductor materials, reduce interface recombination, and improve the photocatalytic degradation of lignin performance. However, when the doping concentration is too high, it may lead to an increase in carrier scattering, but reduce the transmission efficiency, and lead to a decrease in photocatalytic activity.

In the figure, the photocurrent density of all samples increases immediately when the light is turned on, and then drops sharply to the initial state after the light is turned off, showing a sensitive photoelectric response ability [26].

In order to investigate the resistance of photogenerated electrons in the transport process, the electrochemical impedance spectra of all catalysts were tested. Generally speaking, the charge-transfer resistance of the catalyst is equivalent to the diameter of the semicircular part of the Nyquist diagram, and the smaller the radius, the lower the charge-transfer resistance [27]. As shown in Fig. 18(a). Compared with CHL > EDA > CHL > EG, CHL as the solvent has a smaller arc radius, but in terms of performance, MeOH as the solvent has the best degradation rate, while CHL is the worst.

The above results indicate that the electrochemical characterization of the materials is not completely corresponding to the photocatalytic degradation activity, because the charge transfer mode and carrier direction of the two materials are different in the reaction determination process. In the process of electrochemical measurement, electrons need to be transferred from the surface of the material to the surface of the electrochemical electrode to convert into detection signals. In the process of photocatalytic degradation, the photogenerated electrons on the surface of CdS and CQDs are transferred to the surface of CdS through heterojunction, which can directly participate in the process of photocatalytic degradation. Therefore, in the process of measuring the transient photocurrent, more electrons need to be transferred through the CdS surface to the electrochemical electrode surface. Some studies have shown that charge may be trapped by defects on the semiconductor surface during the transfer process [28], resulting in reduced impedance. At the same time, due to the suitable band location and photoconductivity of CdS/CQDs, it can receive and disperse the holes of the system, thus inhibiting the recombination of carriers. Therefore, in general, CdS reduces the transport efficiency of photogenerated electrons in the electrochemical process, while CQDs promotes carrier dispersion and inhibits carrier recombination. In the photocatalytic process, CdS and CQDs enhance the photocatalytic lignin degradation activity because of the synergistic effect on accelerating charge transfer to promote carrier separation.

As shown in Fig. 18(b), CdS synthesized at different hydrothermal temperatures has the smallest radius of 200 °C and the largest radius of 180 °C. This corresponds to the degradation performance of the lignin. It is proved that CdS 200 °C has lower interfacial electron transfer resistance. The maximum transient photocurrent, the sub-small arc radius and the best decolorization curve of CdS 200 °C can also confirm that CdS 200 °C has the best photogenerated electron-hole separation ability and the sub-small electron transfer resistance [29]. These properties promote the optimal photocatalytic activity of CdS at 200 °C.

For convenience, Fig. 18(a)–(c)examines the effects of different CQDs doping ratios, with the right amount of CQDs doping introducing extra free electrons into the semiconductor, and these extra charge carriers increase the material's electrical conductivity, resulting in lower resistivity [30].

Figs. 19 and 20
show the decolorization curves of degradation curves of SLS and AL depolymerized by each photocatalyst under different conditions under visible light excitation.
As can be seen from Fig. 19(a) AL degradation curve, when the hydrothermal temperature is 180 °C, the degradation rate is in the order of MeOH > EDA > EG > CHL.
It can also be seen from the SLS degradation curve in Fig. 19(b) that when the hydrothermal temperature is 180 °C, the solvent is MeOH > EG > EDA > CHL.
MeOH has the best effect when it is the solvent, which is related to the strong polarity of MeOH, which corresponds to the previous characterization conclusions.

In Figs. 19(b)–20(b), the degradation curves of SLS and AL were compared at different hydrothermal temperatures under the condition that the solvent was MeOH. It can be seen that the degradation rate was 200 °C > 180 °C > 220 °C.

This is because while the reaction rate usually increases as the temperature increases (according to the Arrhenius equation), there is an optimal temperature range for the catalyst. Beyond this range, further temperature increases may cause the reaction rate to decline instead. This is due to the excessive temperature that makes the reactant molecules too active, resulting in collisions between them that are no longer efficient, resulting in a change in the reaction path [31].

The concentration of CQDs doping was changed under the condition of solvent MeOH and temperature of 200 °C.
(1)
The degradation rate of AL in Fig. 19(b) can be seen as CdS/CQDs-1 (1 ml CQDs doping) > CdS/CQDs-2 (2 ml CQDs doping) > CdS/CQDs-0.5 (0.5 ml CQDs doping) > CdS. Among them, the optimal AL effect reaches 100 % when 1mlCQDs is added.
(2) Fig. 20(b) SLS degradation It can be seen from the degradation curve that CdS/CQDs-1>CdS/CQDs-2>CdS/CQDs-0.5>CdS. When 1 ml CQDs is added, the optimal SLS degradation effect reaches 90 %.

It can be found that the addition of CQDs has a nonlinear effect on the degradation rate. At low concentration, CQDs act as an effective active center to promote the reaction. However, with the increase of concentration, too much CQDs will lead to excessive coverage of the active site on the catalyst surface or the formation of unfavorable structures, thereby reducing the degradation rate.

In addition, the kinetics of SLS and AL decolorization with all photocatalysts were studied and analyzed. As shown in Figs. 19(d)–20(d), all the fitting kinetic processes of photocatalysis conform to the quasi-first-order kinetic fitting curve.

Fig. 21 shows the decolorization curve of CdS photocatalyst for SLS and AL at 190–500 nm. It can be seen from the diagram that the UV absorption peak at 275 nm decreases first and then remains unchanged with the extension of reaction time. The core is that when light shines on the surface of the photocatalyst, the catalyst can absorb photon energy and generate electron-hole pairs. These electrons and holes can then participate in the REDOX reaction, thereby promoting the transformation or degradation of the target substance [32].

The degradation effects of some reported photocatalysts on natural lignin were summarized, and comparisons were made in terms of substrate concentration, catalyst dosage, light source type, substrate source and reaction time (Table 2). Although the initial experimental conditions in each case are not exactly the same, they can still be compared. It can be found that the commonly used photocatalysts at present generally have a relatively high degradation efficiency under ultraviolet light, with some even reaching 99.24 %, but the required time is relatively long, up to 240 min. However, the lignin is generally low under visible light irradiation conditions. The degradation efficiency of CdS/CQD-1s for SLS can reach over 90 %, and for AL, it can degrade to 100 %. Among them, the degradation effect of alkaline lignin is particularly prominent, and the degradation can be completed in about 60 min. Although the reaction time for degrading SLS is relatively long, the light source in this study was visible light and the amount of catalyst used was relatively low. The degradation effect was still higher than that of other photocatalysts. Overall, the three-dimensional CdS/CQDs composite has strong competitiveness in the photocatalytic degradation of lignin.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability of catalyst before and after light reaction
ce:section.ce:para: The stability and structural changes of the photocatalyst before and after the reaction were detected, as shown in Fig. 22 (a)–(b). After eight cycles, the catalytic morphology remained unchanged as shown in Fig. 22 (c)-(d). After eight cycles, the activity of the photocatalyst decreased to a certain extent, but it still had advantages compared with pure-phase CdS. Although the band gap and morphology of CdS were adjusted in this paper, the photocorrosion during cycling still has a certain influence on the performance of the catalyst. It can be seen from the XRD patterns Fig. 22(e) before and after illumination that the peak intensities on both sides of the peak weaken after photoetching. This is because Cd2+ ions can easily precipitate during the photocatalytic process, thereby destroying the crystal structure. Long-term exposure to light will cause the degradation of CdS crystals. However, the infrared spectra of CdS/CQDs after eight cycles are shown in Fig. 22(f). There was no change, which proved that the active sites of the photocatalyst still maintained high activity after multiple cycles. [41]. Secondly, the by-products or intermediates generated during the reaction may be adsorbed on the catalyst surface during light irradiation, which occupy the active site, resulting in the degradation of catalyst cycling performance [42].

In order to study the photocatalytic degradation mechanism of the CdS/CQDs-1 photocatalyst, we first conducted a capture experiment to determine the main active free radicals in the photocatalytic process. PBQ, Na2C2O4, IPA and potassium bromate were used as the scavenger of cathode radicals (·O2 −), holes (h+) and hydroxyl radicals (·OH) electrons (e−) of superoxides respectively. Fig. 23 (a)–(b) shows the effects of CdS/CQDs-1 samples after adding different collectors on the photocatalytic degradation efficiency of AL and SLS. Without the addition of the collector, the degradation rate of the CdS/CQDs-1 composite material can reach 90 % within 180 min, and the degradation rate of AL can reach 100 %. When sodium oxalate was added as the scavenger, the degradation rate of AL was 29.4 %. The degradation efficiency of SLS is 53 %, indicating that holes have a significant impact on the degradation of AL. When PBQ (p-benzoquinone) is added as a superoxide radical catcher, it can be directly observed that the degradation rate of SLS is significantly reduced. After the addition of p-benzoquinone, the degradation rate of SLS was 22 %, while that of AL was only 15 %, indicating that superoxide radicals play a crucial role in the photocatalytic degradation of lignin. When IPA is used as a hydroxyl radical scavenger the photocatalytic degradation efficiency decrease compared with that without the addition of the scavenger, but it is not obvious. Subsequently, the free radical capture experiment (EPR) was conducted for verification. When potassium bromate was added for electron capture, there was basically no effect, indicating that electrons did not play any role in the photocatalytic degradation process.

In order to further confirm the active substances, spin resonance (EPR) detection was performed on the CdS/CQDs-1 samples to verify the results of the capture experiments. As shown in Fig. 24 (a)–(c), no DMPO·O2 − and DMPO·OH signals were detected under dark conditions.

Under visible light irradiation of the sample, DMPO·O2 − and holes respectively exhibited typical hexets, and the appearance of triple characteristic peaks indicated that ·O2 − and holes were generated in the cd/CQDs-1 system after light irradiation, while no standard quartet peak appeared in DMPO·OH after light irradiation, which was consistent with the capture results. Hydroxyl radicals do not function in photocatalytic degradation. Holes and superoxide radicals play a major role in the degradation system.
The roles of h+, ·O2 − and ·OH radicals in photocatalytic degradation were further proved.

Total ion current map of SLS
ce:section.ce:para: The total ion current profile of the SLS degradation products is shown in Fig. 25.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible degradation paths during SLS degradation

ce:list-item.ce:para: As shown in Figs. 26 and 1 a is the degradation product of lignin degraded by CdS/CQDs-1 for 30min. The π electron cloud at the end CC of the benzene ring side chain of the intermediate product 1a has high density, relatively exposed position and relatively unstable [43]. Due to the blocking effect of methoxy group, the double bond at γ position will be rearranged to β position to obtain product 1b, and the product 1c will be further oxidized under the action of superoxide free radicals, and the final product 1e will be obtained by addition and dehydration of 1c with lignin monomer 1d [44].

As shown in Fig. 27 , by the electron transfer between the aromatic ring and the lignin monomer 2a, a radical cationic intermediate 2b is produced. The intermediates were further photocatalyzed to break the Cα-Cβ bond to produce aromatic aldehyde 2c and carbon free radical, which were esterified with lignin monomer 2e to produce the final product 2f [45].

As shown in Fig. 28 , The lignin monomer through the photocatalytic β cleavage of the Cα-Cβ bond, a semi-acetal reaction occurs to produce aromatic 3b vanillin and 3a phenol-based compounds, and a ring reaction produces the final product 3d [46].

As shown in Fig. 29 , The oxidation of lignin monomer 4a, benzene ring intersite and parasite changes the electron cloud density of benzene ring large π bond, resulting in large π bond rearrangement. Produces the final product 4b [46].

ce:section.ce:para: The total ion current profile of AL degradation products is shown in Fig. 30.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible degradation paths in the degradation of AL

ce:list-item.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 31 , Aromatic aldehydes and phenol derivatives are produced by the photocatalytic cleavage of Cα-Cβ bond in a1 and b1, and oxidation reactions under the action of superoxide free radicals. b1 is oxidized to aromatic ketone under the action of superoxide radical, and aldehydes and ketones undergo condensation reaction to produce the final degradation product e1 [46].

As shown in Fig. 32 , Hydrolysis of AL in water 2a → 2c Reaction with intermediates during hydrolysis of AL leads to the breaking of the C β -OAr bond in d1 to produce e2, which further generates a stable carboanion intermediate f2, and the clever molecular rearrangement of base-promoted d1 leads to the cleavage of the resulting Cα–CH–AQ bond. Release of two phenol anions (g2 and h2) after cleavage of the C β -OAr bond [46].
As shown in Fig. 33 . The lignin monomer a3 and b3 were esterified to produce c3 [45].

ce:section.ce:para: In this paper, the optimal temperature is selected by adjusting the morphology of CdS. The CdS/CQDs heterojunction catalytic material was successfully synthesized by adopting the CQDs loading strategy. The narrow band gap of CdS enables it to have strong absorption in the visible light region, which is significantly superior to TiO2 and ZnO that only respond to ultraviolet light. The introduction of CQDs can convert near-infrared rays into high-energy visible light, broaden the light absorption range, improve the utilization rate of sunlight, increase the active sites of photocatalytic reactions, enhance the transfer rate of photogenerated carriers, significantly reduce the recombination rate of holes and carriers. At the same time, and the three-dimensional structure of microspheres provides a continuous electron transport path, enhancing the charge mobility. It plays a positive role in the photocatalytic degradation process. The micron-sized spherical structure is convenient for centrifugation or filtration recovery and is easier to separate compared with the nano-powder catalyst g-C3N4. Compared with the currently common photocatalyst TiO2/ZnO: it relies on ultraviolet light (accounting for only 5 % of sunlight), and its practical application is limited; g-C3N4: high carrier recombination rate, low specific surface area, and requires complex modification; MOFs: poor water stability, narrow light response range, and relatively high cost; precious metal catalysts: they are costly and prone to poisoning and inactivation. CQDs can be prepared from biomass carbon sources. The CdS synthesis process is mature and the overall cost is lower than that of precious metal modified catalysts. CQDs significantly broadens the visible light absorption range of CdS through surface state regulation and upconversion effects, making it more suitable for the spectral conditions required for lignin degradation. CQDs coating the surface of CdS effectively inhibits the recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs, slows down photocorrosion, and improves the cycling stability of the material. CdS/CQDs composites can still maintain high activity after multiple cycles, while pure CdS is prone to deactivation.

The core advantages of CdS photocatalysts lie in their low cost, visible light responsiveness, structural tunability and efficient carrier management capabilities. Combined with strategies such as heterostructure construction and co-catalyst loading, significant performance improvements can be achieved. Although its photocorrosiveness and toxicity (such as Cd2+ pollution) still need to be further addressed, it still shows significant industrial application potential in the fields of photocatalytic hydrogen production and environmental governance. In the future, through green synthesis methods (such as template-free methods and low-temperature solution methods) and composite modification strategies, it is expected to further promote its commercialization process. The discovery of CdS/CQDs photocatalysts provides a new approach for enhancing the photocatalytic degradation activity of sulfur-containing compounds.
